caption:
Entrepreneur and business strategist Daniel Priestley recently issued a stark warning on The Diary of a CEO about a looming AI financial crash in 2029. He argues that the global economy is pouring roughly 650 billion dollars annually into massive data centers that currently lack a sustainable profit model. Unlike the railroads of the 19th century that lasted for a hundred years, modern data center hardware becomes obsolete every three to four years, creating a relentless and expensive cycle of debt and replacement.

Priestley compares this infrastructure bubble to the market distortions that occurred exactly a century ago, leading to the Great Depression. He notes that while billions are spent on the "supply" side of intelligence, only about 5% of users are currently willing to pay for premium AI services. This imbalance suggests a massive correction is coming where the "old world" financial systems will struggle to support the hyper-efficiency of AI, potentially wiping out retirement savings and traditional corporate structures that fail to adapt.

Is the 2029 crash an inevitable result of our AI infrastructure obsession?

Follow @future.is.ai

#fyp #life #ai
audio_track_actual: Audio excerpt of Daniel Priestley speaking on Steven Bartlett's The Diary of a CEO podcast (March 2026 episode), packaged here over title-card graphics by @future.is.ai.
video_overlay_text_observed: Title-card overlays naming Daniel Priestley, the Diary of a CEO source, and the years referenced in his analysis (the 19th-century railway booms, 1929, 2029); split-screen graphics comparing infrastructure-lifecycle figures for railroads, highways, fiber, and data centers.
audio_content_summary: Priestley argues that whenever a society spends more than 3 percent of GDP on an infrastructure buildout, a roughly decade-long economic bust has historically followed. He cites the UK and U.S. railway booms, electrification, and highway buildouts as precedents. He notes a structural difference with the current AI capex wave: data-center hardware obsolesces in 3–4 years, versus a century for rail, fifty years for highways, and thirty for fiber. He predicts a major financial meltdown in 2029, the centennial of the Great Depression. The underlying capex numbers — roughly $650 billion globally per year, ~5 percent of U.S. GDP — are corroborated by Goldman Sachs, Citi, the BIS, and Dell'Oro Group; the 2029 prediction is Priestley's analytical inference.
implied_frame: The reel pairs Priestley's prediction with the implication that the current AI buildout follows the structure of every previous infrastructure bubble — but with a far shorter hardware lifecycle. The Goldman, BIS, and Citi numbers cited below confirm the scale; the bust prediction is the speaker's interpretive call.
capture_method: Headless browser recording of the in-page <video> element via MediaRecorder over captureStream(), viewport 1280x800.
